topics.name: Inception of Java and Platform Independence
subtopics: Invention of Java — history, creators, why Java was needed
Features of Java — platform independence, OOP, robust, secure, multithreaded
Platform Dependency vs Platform Independence — C failure, Java success
Java versions timeline — Java 8, 11, 17, 21 key features
required_content: Java invented by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems in 1995. Originally called 'Oak', renamed Java. Designed for embedded systems, became internet language
Platform Independence: C code compiles to machine-specific binary. Java compiles to bytecode (.class) — JVM interprets bytecode on any OS. WORA: Write Once Run Anywhere
C failure: C program compiled on Windows won't run on Linux — different machine instructions. Java solution: JVM acts as universal interpreter layer on every OS
Key Java features: Object-Oriented, Platform Independent, Robust (strong type checking), Secure (no pointers), Multithreaded, Garbage Collected
required_diagrams: Platform independence diagram: C flow (Source.c → Windows Compiler → Windows .exe → runs only on Windows). Java flow (Source.java → Java Compiler → Bytecode .class → JVM on Windows/Linux/Mac → runs everywhere). Side by side comparison.
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | C Language | Java || Compilation target | Machine code | Bytecode (.class) || Portability | Not portable | Platform independent (WORA) || Memory management | Manual (malloc/free) | Automatic (Garbage Collector) || Pointers | Yes | No (references only) || Paradigm | Procedural | Object-Oriented
common_mistakes: Thinking Java source code runs directly on JVM — source code first compiles to bytecode, JVM runs bytecode
Confusing JDK, JRE, JVM — JDK = JRE + development tools. JRE = JVM + libraries. JVM = bytecode interpreter

topics.name: Object-Orientation Fundamentals and First Java Program
subtopics: Object-Orientation principles — objects in real life vs in Java
Class vs Object — blueprint vs instance
new keyword — object creation and memory allocation
Main method syntax — public static void main(String[] args)
Develop, Compile and Execute a Java Program
required_content: Class = blueprint/template. Object = instance created from blueprint. Student class defines structure. new Student() creates actual object in heap memory
4 principles of OOP: Encapsulation (data hiding), Inheritance (reuse), Polymorphism (many forms), Abstraction (hide complexity)
new keyword: allocates memory in heap, calls constructor, returns reference. Student s = new Student() — s holds reference (address), not the object itself
main method is entry point. public: accessible from outside. static: no object needed to call. void: no return. String[] args: command-line arguments
required_diagrams: Class vs Object diagram: Left side shows Student class as blueprint with fields (name, rollNo, marks) and methods (displayInfo). Right side shows two separate Student objects (s1: Alice, 101 and s2: Bob, 102) in heap memory with arrows from class to objects showing instantiation.
common_mistakes: Confusing class and object — class is defined once, objects are created many times from it
Thinking Student s1 = new Student() stores the object in s1 — s1 stores only the REFERENCE (memory address)

topics.name: Input/Output and Command-Line Arguments
subtopics: System.out vs System.err — standard output vs error stream
Scanner class — reading input from keyboard
Command-Line Arguments — passing data from CLI
Naming conventions in Java — camelCase, PascalCase
required_content: System.out.println() — standard output to console. System.err.println() — error output (shown in red in IDEs). Both are PrintStream objects
Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in). sc.nextInt(), sc.nextDouble(), sc.next() (word), sc.nextLine() (full line). Always close scanner
Command-line args: java MyProgram Alice 25 — args[0]="Alice", args[1]="25". All args are Strings, parse with Integer.parseInt() etc
Java naming: class names PascalCase (StudentInfo), method/variable names camelCase (firstName), constants UPPER_CASE (MAX_SIZE)
common_mistakes: Calling sc.nextLine() after sc.nextInt() — nextInt() leaves newline in buffer, nextLine() reads empty string. Fix: add sc.nextLine() to consume the newline
Forgetting to parse command-line args — args[] is always String[], use Integer.parseInt() for numbers

topics.name: Data Types, Type Casting and Literals
subtopics: Primitive data types — integer types (byte, short, int, long), real types (float, double)
Character and boolean data types
Implicit vs Explicit type casting
Type conversion chart — widening vs narrowing
var keyword in Java — local type inference
required_content: 8 primitive types: byte(1B), short(2B), int(4B), long(8B), float(4B), double(8B), char(2B), boolean(1b). Default for integers: int. Default for decimals: double
Widening (implicit): byte→short→int→long→float→double. Happens automatically. Narrowing (explicit): must cast manually — data loss possible. double d=9.8; int i=(int)d; → i=9
char in Java is 2 bytes (Unicode, not ASCII). char c='A'; — stores Unicode value 65. Can do arithmetic: 'A'+1 = 66 = 'B'
var keyword (Java 10+): var x = 10; — compiler infers int. var list = new ArrayList<String>(); — cleaner code. Cannot use var for method parameters or fields
required_tables: Primitive data types: Type | Size | Range | Default | Example || byte | 1B | -128 to 127 | 0 | byte b = 100 || short | 2B | -32768 to 32767 | 0 | short s = 30000 || int | 4B | -2.1B to 2.1B | 0 | int i = 42 || long | 8B | very large | 0L | long l = 100L || float | 4B | 6-7 decimal digits | 0.0f | float f = 3.14f || double | 8B | 15-16 decimal digits | 0.0 | double d = 3.14 || char | 2B | 0 to 65535 | '\u0000' | char c = 'A' || boolean | 1bit | true/false | false | boolean b = true
common_mistakes: Forgetting L suffix for long literals — long l = 9999999999 causes compile error. Write 9999999999L
Forgetting f suffix for float — float f = 3.14 causes error because 3.14 is double by default. Write 3.14f

topics.name: Operators in Java
subtopics: Arithmetic operators — +, -, *, /, %, ++, --
Comparison and logical operators — ==, !=, >, <, &&, ||, !
Bitwise operators — &, |, ^, ~, <<, >>
Ternary operator and instanceof
Operator precedence
required_content: Integer division: 7/2 = 3 (not 3.5). One operand must be double/float for decimal result: 7.0/2 = 3.5 or (double)7/2 = 3.5
% (modulo): 7%3 = 1 (remainder). Useful for even/odd check (n%2==0), circular indexing, last digit (n%10)
Short-circuit: && stops at first false. || stops at first true. Avoids NullPointerException: if(obj != null && obj.value > 0)
Bitwise: & (AND), | (OR), ^ (XOR), ~ (NOT), << (left shift = multiply by 2), >> (right shift = divide by 2)
common_mistakes: Using = instead of == in conditions — if(x = 5) assigns 5 to x, doesn't compare. Java catches this for primitives but not booleans
Assuming & and && behave the same — & always evaluates both sides, && short-circuits. Use && for null checks

topics.name: Conditional and Looping Statements
subtopics: if, if-else, if-else-if, nested-if statements
Switch statement — allowed types, fall-through, enhanced switch (Java 14+)
for, while, do-while loops
break, continue — labelled and unlabelled
required_content: Switch allowed types (Java): byte, short, int, char, String, enum. NOT: long, float, double, boolean
Fall-through in switch: without break, execution continues to next case. Sometimes intentional (multiple cases same action)
do-while executes body at least once — condition checked AFTER first execution. Use for menu-driven programs
Labelled break: breaks out of outer loop. Labelled continue: continues outer loop. Useful for nested loop control
common_mistakes: Using float/double in switch — compile error. Switch only accepts int-compatible types and String
Missing break in switch — unintentional fall-through causes multiple cases to execute

topics.name: JVM Architecture and Variable Types
subtopics: JDK vs JRE vs JVM vs JIT — hierarchy and responsibilities
JVM Architecture — ClassLoader, Memory Areas, Execution Engine
JVM Memory Areas — Heap, Stack, Method Area, PC Register
Local Variables vs Instance Variables — scope, lifetime, defaults
required_content: JDK = JRE + development tools (javac, javadoc, jdb). JRE = JVM + class libraries. JVM = ClassLoader + Memory + Execution Engine. JIT = Just-In-Time compiler inside JVM
JVM Memory: Heap (objects, instance variables — GC managed). Stack (method calls, local variables — LIFO). Method Area (class metadata, static variables). PC Register (current instruction address)

Local variables: declared inside method, no default value (must initialize), exist only during method execution. Instance variables: declared in class, have default values (int=0, String=null, boolean=false)
Life cycle of Java program: Source.java → javac → Source.class (bytecode) → JVM loads → ClassLoader → Bytecode Verifier → Interpreter/JIT → CPU
required_diagrams: JVM Architecture diagram: show JVM as a box containing three sections. 1) ClassLoader subsystem (loading, linking, initialization). 2) Memory Areas (Heap, Stack, Method Area, PC Register, Native Method Stack). 3) Execution Engine (Interpreter, JIT Compiler, Garbage Collector). Show .class file entering ClassLoader.
required_tables: Variable comparison: Feature | Local Variable | Instance Variable | Static Variable || Declared in | Method body | Class body | Class body with static || Memory | Stack | Heap (per object) | Method Area (once) || Default value | None (must init) | Yes (0, null, false) | Yes || Lifetime | Method execution | Object lifetime | Class lifetime || Access | Within method only | Through object | Through class name
common_mistakes: Using local variable without initialization — compile error 'variable might not have been initialized'
Thinking JVM is the same on all platforms — JVM implementation varies per OS (that's why Java is platform independent)

topics.name: Methods — Definition, Types and Overloading
subtopics: Method definition — syntax, return type, parameters
4 types of methods — no param/no return, no param/with return, param/no return, param/with return
Pass-by-Value vs Pass-by-Reference in Java
Method Overloading — same name different parameters
Wrapper classes — boxing, unboxing, autoboxing
required_content: Method signature = method name + parameter types. Return type is NOT part of signature. Two methods with same signature = compile error
Java is ALWAYS pass-by-value. For primitives: copy of value passed. For objects: copy of REFERENCE (address) passed — object itself can be modified but reassignment doesn't affect original
Method overloading: same name, different parameter count or types. Resolved at compile time (static/compile-time polymorphism). Return type alone cannot distinguish overloaded methods
Wrapper classes: Integer, Double, Character, Boolean etc. Boxing: int → Integer (new Integer(5)). Unboxing: Integer → int. Autoboxing: Java does it automatically (Java 5+)
common_mistakes: Thinking Java uses pass-by-reference for objects — Java passes a COPY of the reference, not the reference itself. Reassigning the parameter won't change the original variable
Confusing method overloading (same class, different params) with method overriding (subclass, same signature)

topics.name: Arrays — Declaration, Initialization and Operations
subtopics: Array declaration and initialization — 1D, 2D, 3D arrays
Regular vs Jagged arrays
For-each loop — syntax, iterating arrays, limitations
Arrays utility class — sort, copyOf, fill, binarySearch
Varargs methods — variable argument count
required_content: Array in Java: int[] arr = new int[5]; — stored in heap. arr.length is property (not method). Index 0 to length-1. ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException if out of range
Jagged array: int[][] jagged = new int[3][]; — rows have different lengths. jagged[0] = new int[2]; jagged[1] = new int[4]; Each row is separate array
for-each limitations: no index access, no backward traversal, cannot modify array elements, cannot skip elements. Use regular for loop when index needed
Varargs: void print(String... args) — zero or more arguments. args is treated as array inside method. Must be last parameter. Cannot have two varargs parameters
common_mistakes: Using arr.length() — compile error. length is a PROPERTY for arrays, length() is a method for Strings
Comparing arrays with == — checks reference equality, not content. Use Arrays.equals() for content comparison

topics.name: Strings — Immutable and Mutable
subtopics: String creation — string literals vs new String()
String immutability — String pool, 10 rules of Strings
String methods — length, charAt, substring, indexOf, replace, split, trim
Mutable Strings — StringBuilder vs StringBuffer
String to primitive and primitive to String conversion
required_content: String is immutable — once created, cannot change. String s = "Hello"; s = s + " World"; — original "Hello" unchanged, new object created. Old one eligible for GC

String pool: String s1 = "Hello"; String s2 = "Hello"; — same object in pool. String s3 = new String("Hello"); — new object in heap. s1==s2 is true, s1==s3 is false. Use .equals() for content comparison
StringBuilder (not thread-safe, faster) vs StringBuffer (thread-safe, slower). Use StringBuilder in single-threaded code. Append, insert, delete, reverse, replace methods
String to int: Integer.parseInt("42"). int to String: String.valueOf(42) or 42+"". String to char array: str.toCharArray()
required_tables: StringBuilder vs StringBuffer vs String: Feature | String | StringBuilder | StringBuffer || Mutability | Immutable | Mutable | Mutable || Thread Safety | Yes | No | Yes || Performance | Slow (for concat) | Fastest | Moderate || Storage | String Pool/Heap | Heap | Heap || Use when | Fixed strings | Single-threaded | Multi-threaded
common_mistakes: Using == to compare String content — always use .equals() for content, == only checks reference
Concatenating Strings in a loop — creates many temporary objects. Use StringBuilder for loop concatenation

topics.name: Encapsulation, Constructors and this Keyword
subtopics: Encapsulation — private fields, public getters/setters
Shadowing problem and this keyword
Constructors — default, parameterized, constructor overloading
Constructor chaining — this() and super()
Constructor vs Method differences
required_content: Encapsulation: private fields cannot be accessed directly outside class. Public getter/setter methods control access. Benefits: validation in setter, read-only fields (no setter), write-only fields (no getter)
Shadowing: when parameter name == instance variable name, parameter shadows the field. this.name refers to instance variable, name refers to parameter
Constructor: same name as class, no return type (not even void). Called automatically on object creation. If no constructor defined, compiler provides default (no-arg) constructor
this() calls another constructor in same class — must be first statement. Used for constructor chaining to avoid code duplication
common_mistakes: Not using this keyword when parameter and field have same name — shadowing bug, field never gets set
Making constructor void — constructors have NO return type, not even void. Writing void BankAccount() is a regular method, not constructor

topics.name: Static Keyword — Variables, Methods, Blocks
subtopics: Static variables — shared across all objects, class-level
Static methods — no object needed, cannot access instance members
Static blocks — class initialization, execution order
Static vs Instance control flow
Singleton Design Pattern using static
required_content: Static members belong to CLASS, not object. All objects share one copy of static variable. Accessed as ClassName.variable without creating object
Static method cannot use this keyword, cannot access instance variables/methods directly. main() is static because JVM calls it without creating an object
Execution order: static blocks (once, when class loads) → instance blocks (every object creation) → constructor
Singleton: only one object of a class. private constructor + private static instance + public static getInstance() method
common_mistakes: Accessing instance variable from static method — compile error. Static method has no 'this', cannot access non-static members directly
Thinking static block runs every time object is created — static block runs only ONCE when class is first loaded

topics.name: Inheritance, Access Modifiers and Packages
subtopics: Inheritance — IS-A relationship, extends keyword, types
Access modifiers — private, default, protected, public
Packages — definition, import statement, static import
Method Overriding — rules, @Override annotation, covariant return
final keyword — final class, method, variable
required_content: Inheritance: child class inherits all non-private members of parent. extends keyword. Java supports single inheritance (one parent class). Multiple inheritance via interfaces
Access levels: private (class only) < default (package) < protected (package + subclass) < public (everywhere)
Method Overriding rules: same name, same parameters, same or covariant return type. @Override annotation — compiler checks correctness. Cannot override final, static, private methods
super() calls parent constructor — must be first line. super.method() calls parent's method. If child has no constructor, compiler adds super() automatically
required_tables: Access modifiers: Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass | Other Package || private | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ || default | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ || protected | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ || public | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅
common_mistakes: Thinking @Override is mandatory for overriding — it's optional but highly recommended. Without it, typos create new methods instead of overriding
Calling super() anywhere except first line of constructor — compile error

topics.name: Polymorphism and Abstraction
subtopics: Polymorphism — compile-time (overloading) vs runtime (overriding)
Abstract classes and abstract methods
Interfaces — rules, default methods, functional interfaces
Lambda expressions — syntax and usage with functional interfaces
Abstract class vs Interface comparison
required_content: Runtime polymorphism: Animal a = new Dog(); a.makeSound() — calls Dog's makeSound() at runtime. Reference type is Animal, object type is Dog. Method resolved at runtime based on actual object
Abstract class: cannot instantiate, can have abstract and concrete methods, can have constructor, can have instance variables. Subclass MUST implement all abstract methods
Interface: all methods implicitly public abstract (before Java 8). Can have default and static methods (Java 8+). All variables implicitly public static final. Class implements interface
Lambda: replaces anonymous inner class for functional interfaces. (params) -> expression. Comparator<String> c = (a,b) -> a.compareTo(b); instead of new Comparator<String>(){...}
required_tables: Abstract class vs Interface: Feature | Abstract Class | Interface || Instantiation | No | No || Constructor | Yes | No || Instance variables | Yes | No (only static final) || Method types | Abstract + concrete | Abstract + default + static || Inheritance | extends (one) | implements (multiple) || When to use | Partial implementation, IS-A | Contract/capability, multiple types
common_mistakes: Trying to instantiate abstract class — Shape s = new Shape() causes compile error
Thinking interface variables can be changed — interface variables are implicitly public static final (constants)

topics.name: Association, Aggregation and Composition
subtopics: IS-A vs HAS-A relationships
Types of Association — one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many
Dependency Injection — constructor injection vs setter injection
Aggregation vs Composition — lifecycle dependency
Delegation Model
required_content: IS-A (inheritance): Dog IS-A Animal. HAS-A (association): Car HAS-A Engine. Use IS-A when subtype relationship exists, HAS-A when ownership/usage relationship exists
Aggregation: weak HAS-A. Department HAS-A Professor — Professor can exist without Department. Composition: strong HAS-A. Car HAS-A Engine — Engine cannot exist without Car (same lifecycle)
Constructor injection: dependency passed via constructor (immutable, tested easily). Setter injection: dependency set via setter (flexible, optional dependencies)
Delegation: object delegates task to another object rather than inheriting. Printer delegates to InkCartridge. Preferred over inheritance when relationship is HAS-A not IS-A
required_tables: Association types: Type | Relationship | Lifecycle | Example || Dependency | Uses temporarily | Independent | Method uses another class || Association | Knows about | Independent | Teacher knows Students || Aggregation | HAS-A (weak) | Independent | Department has Professors || Composition | HAS-A (strong) | Dependent | Car has Engine
common_mistakes: Using inheritance when association is more appropriate — if relationship is HAS-A not IS-A, use composition
Confusing aggregation and composition — key difference is lifecycle: composition parts die with the whole, aggregation parts survive

topics.name: Exception Handling — Complete Guide
subtopics: Exception hierarchy — Throwable, Error vs Exception
try-catch-finally — execution flow
Checked vs Unchecked exceptions
throw vs throws keywords
Custom exceptions and try-with-resources
required_content: Exception hierarchy: Throwable → Error (JVM problems, don't catch) and Exception (application problems). Exception → RuntimeException (unchecked) and checked exceptions
Checked exceptions: must handle or declare (IOException, SQLException). Unchecked: RuntimeException subclasses (NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException) — optional handling
throw: explicitly throw an exception object. throws: declares method might throw checked exception — callers must handle
try-with-resources (Java 7): resources in try() automatically closed. No need for finally to close. Resource must implement AutoCloseable
required_tables: Checked vs Unchecked: Feature | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception || Superclass | Exception | RuntimeException || Must handle | Yes | No || Examples | IOException, SQLException | NullPointerException, ArithmeticException || When occurs | External resources | Programming errors || Detection | Compile time | Runtime
common_mistakes: Catching Exception or Throwable for everything — too broad, hides real errors and catches things you can't handle
Empty catch block — silently swallows exceptions, makes debugging impossible. Always at minimum log the exception

topics.name: Multithreading — Thread Lifecycle and Synchronization
subtopics: Creating threads — Thread class vs Runnable interface
Thread lifecycle — New, Runnable, Running, Waiting, Dead
Race condition and synchronization — synchronized keyword
join() and isAlive() methods
Daemon threads and Deadlock
required_content: Two ways: extend Thread class (single inheritance limitation) or implement Runnable (preferred — flexible). Lambda for Runnable: new Thread(() -> { /* code */ }).start()
Thread states: New (created) → Runnable (start() called) → Running (CPU allocated) → Waiting/Sleeping → Dead (run() completed). Cannot restart dead thread
Race condition: multiple threads modify shared resource simultaneously → unpredictable results. synchronized keyword ensures only one thread executes block at a time
Deadlock: Thread A holds lock1, wants lock2. Thread B holds lock2, wants lock1. Neither can proceed. Prevention: always acquire locks in same order
common_mistakes: Calling run() instead of start() — run() executes in current thread (no multithreading). start() creates new thread and calls run()
Synchronizing on different objects — synchronized(this) in two different objects doesn't prevent race condition. Use shared lock object

topics.name: Collections Framework and Generics
subtopics: Collections hierarchy — List, Set, Queue interfaces
ArrayList vs LinkedList — when to use which
HashSet, LinkedHashSet, TreeSet — uniqueness and ordering
HashMap, LinkedHashMap, TreeMap
Iterating collections — for-each, Iterator, forEach()
Generics — type safety, bounded wildcards
required_content: Collections framework: List (ordered, duplicates allowed), Set (unordered, no duplicates), Queue (FIFO). ArrayList: fast random access O(1). LinkedList: fast insertion/deletion O(1) at known position
HashSet: no order, O(1) operations. LinkedHashSet: insertion order maintained. TreeSet: sorted order, O(log n). All implement Set — no duplicates
HashMap: no order, O(1) get/put. LinkedHashMap: insertion order. TreeMap: sorted by key. Iterate with entrySet(): for(Map.Entry<K,V> e : map.entrySet())
Generics: List<String> prevents adding wrong types at compile time. Without generics, ClassCastException at runtime. <T extends Number> — bounded type parameter
required_tables: Collections comparison: Class | Order | Duplicates | Null | Performance | Use when || ArrayList | Insertion order | Yes | Yes | O(1) get, O(n) insert middle | Random access, iteration || LinkedList | Insertion order | Yes | Yes | O(1) insert/delete | Frequent insert/delete || HashSet | No order | No | One null | O(1) | Unique elements, fast lookup || TreeSet | Sorted | No | No | O(log n) | Sorted unique elements || HashMap | No order | Keys no | One null key | O(1) | Key-value, fast lookup || TreeMap | Sorted by key | Keys no | No null key | O(log n) | Sorted key-value pairs
common_mistakes: Using ArrayList when you need unique elements — use HashSet. ArrayList allows duplicates
Modifying collection while iterating with for-each — ConcurrentModificationException. Use Iterator's remove() method instead
